steps.title: What is 3D Modelling?
steps.content:
What is 3D Modelling?

3D modelling is the process of creating a digital three-dimensional object on a computer. Engineers, architects, animators and game designers use 3D software every day to design and test objects before they are built or released.

Where is 3D modelling used?

  • Games — every character, weapon, building and environment is a 3D model
  • Animation & film — Pixar, DreamWorks and Marvel all use 3D modelling
  • Architecture — visualising buildings before construction
  • 3D printing — turning digital models into real physical objects
  • Product design — designing phones, cars, furniture

In this course you will use Blockbench — a free, open-source 3D modelling tool that runs right in your browser. It is the same tool used to create models for Minecraft, Roblox, and many indie games.

Which of these is NOT something that uses 3D modelling?

What type of 3D models is Blockbench best known for?

steps.title: The Blockbench Interface
steps.content:
The Blockbench Interface

When Blockbench opens you will see a dark-themed editor. Open a Generic Model (click Create New on the start screen and choose Generic Model) so you have access to all the modelling tools.

Key areas to find:

  • ① Menu bar (top left) — File, Edit, Format menus
  • ② Mode tabs (top centre) — Edit Mode, Paint Mode, Animate Mode
  • ③ 3D Viewport (centre) — where your model lives
  • ④ Outliner (right) — lists every object in the scene
  • ⑤ Properties panel (right below) — position, size, rotation of selected object
  • ⑥ Toolbar (top of viewport) — add cube, add mesh, select, move, resize tools

Navigating the 3D view

  • Orbit (rotate view) — right-click and drag
  • Zoom — scroll wheel
  • Pan — middle-click and drag
  • Reset view — press Numpad 0 or use the view cube

Open the editor below, create a Generic Model, and spend a couple of minutes finding all six areas.

steps.title: Add Your First Cube
steps.content:
Add Your First Cube

In Blockbench the fundamental building block is the Cube (also called a cuboid). Every shape starts as a cube — you then resize, move and combine cubes to build your model.

How to add a cube

  1. Make sure you are in Edit Mode (click the Edit Mode tab at the top)
  2. Click the Add Cube button in the toolbar above the viewport (cube icon with a +)
  3. A cube appears in the centre of the viewport and in the Outliner on the right
  4. Click the cube to select it — it highlights orange
  5. Look at the Properties panel (bottom right) — you can see its position and size

💡 Tip — Undo

Press Ctrl+Z to undo. Press Ctrl+A to select all objects.

steps.title: Resize and Position
steps.content:
Resize and Position Your Cube

A default cube is a 8×8×8 unit box. To build anything useful you need to resize it and move it into position.

Two ways to resize and move

Method 1 — Properties panel (precise)

  • Select the cube
  • In the Properties panel on the right, type exact values into Size (width, height, depth) and Position

Method 2 — Gizmo handles (drag)

  • Select the cube, then choose the Move or Resize tool from the toolbar
  • Coloured handles appear — drag the red handle for X, green for Y, blue for Z

Your task

Resize your cube to be wide and flat — like the floor or a wall of a building. Use the Properties panel to set the size, then check.

steps.title: Build With Multiple Cubes
steps.content:
Build With Multiple Cubes

Real models are built by combining many cubes together. Each cube is an independent object you can move, resize and delete individually.

Build a simple L-shape or T-shape

  1. Add a first cube — make it wide and flat (e.g. a base)
  2. Add a second cube — make it tall and narrow (e.g. a wall or pillar)
  3. Use the Move tool or Properties panel to position the second cube on top of or beside the first
  4. Check the Outliner — you should see two cubes listed

💡 Selecting multiple cubes

Hold Ctrl and click to select multiple cubes at once. Then you can move them all together.

steps.title: Check Your Knowledge
steps.content:
Check Your Knowledge

Answer these questions about what you have learnt.

Which mouse action rotates the view in Blockbench?

What is the basic building block in Blockbench called?

Where do you type exact size and position values for a selected cube?

You want to select three cubes at once. Which key do you hold?

steps.title: Practice: Build a House
steps.content:
Practice: Build a Simple House

Use everything you have learned to build a simple house from cubes.

🏠 Challenge — A house made of cubes

  • 1 wide, tall cube — the main walls of the house
  • 1 flat, wide cube — the roof (position it on top, make it slightly wider)
  • 1 small, thin cube — a chimney sticking up from the roof

Tip: use the Properties panel to set exact sizes. Position each piece by typing coordinates or dragging the move handles. The Outliner should show 3 separate cubes when you are done.

Extension — if you finish early

  • Add window cubes — thin flat slabs positioned on the front face of the walls
  • Add a door — a taller thin slab at the base of the front wall
  • Switch to Paint Mode and colour different parts of the house
